senses.definition: to take advantage of a position to make oneself rich, particularly by using unfair or dishonest methods

examples.x: The corrupt politician used his position to feather his own nest by accepting bribes.
She manipulated company policies to feather her nest by securing lucrative contracts for her own benefit.
The CEO exploited the company's resources to feather his nest, leading to financial losses for the organization.
He took advantage of his connections to feather his own nest by securing exclusive deals and accumulating personal wealth.
The corrupt politician was constantly feathering his own nest by accepting bribes and kickbacks.

word_list.pronunciation: hˈoʊld ɐ ðə kˈændəl tə ðə dˈɛvəl

senses.definition: to be comparable to someone who is very evil
to gain advantage over others by making use of one's contacts and influence in an unfair way

examples.x: He was able to get that job so quickly because he pulled some strings with his uncle, who works in the company.
The politician pulled strings to secure funding for the new community center.
Even though he had no prior experience, he got the promotion because he pulled some strings with the higher-ups.
She managed to pull some strings and get us VIP tickets to the concert.

word_list.word: {not} [do] (any|) evil that good (may|might|) come (out|) of it
word_list.pronunciation: nˌɑːt dˈuː ˌɛni ɔːɹ ˈiːvəl ðæt ɡˈʊd mˈeɪ ɔːɹ mˌaɪt ɔːɹ kˈʌm ɔːɹ ɹɪzˈʌlt ˈaʊt ɔːɹ ʌv ɪt

senses.definition: to avoid doing something bad or unethical for the sake of achieving something good

examples.x: Ethical leaders understand the importance of not compromising their values, even when pressured to do so, as they believe in not doing evil that good may come out of it.

senses.definition: about to do something bad or evil

examples.x: I saw those kids hanging out near the abandoned building again; they must be up to no good.
Whenever he gets that secretive look on his face, you know he's up to no good.
The security guard noticed a group of people loitering around the parking lot late at night and suspected they were up to no good.
The teacher caught the students passing notes during the exam and knew they were up to no good.
She had a feeling that her coworker's sudden interest in the company's financial records meant he was up to no good.

senses.definition: to try to impress someone using insincere praise for personal gain

examples.x: The employee brought coffee for the entire team in an attempt to curry favor with colleagues.

senses.definition: to use various dishonest or complicated methods in order to gain things, particularly in politics or business

examples.x: To succeed in the stock market, you need to know how to wheel and deal effectively.

senses.definition: to be placed in a difficult position by believing something that is not true or trusting someone who lies

examples.x: She fell into a trap set by online scammers who promised easy money.
The company fell into a trap when they trusted the competitor's false information.
He realized too late that he had fallen into a trap of his own making when he borrowed money from a loan shark.

senses.definition: someone on whom scientific experiments are tested

examples.x: The new drug is still in its testing phase, and they need volunteers to be guinea pigs for the clinical trials.
She felt like a guinea pig when her company implemented a new management system without warning.
Using students as guinea pigs for educational experiments without their consent is unethical.
They turned their backyard into a guinea pig sanctuary, adopting animals that had been used in laboratory experiments.
The restaurant decided to make its customers guinea pigs by offering a new experimental menu item.

senses.definition: to secretly offer someone money or anything of high value in order to persuade them to help one or do what one desires

examples.x: Corrupt officials were known for greasing their palms to turn a blind eye to illegal activities.

senses.definition: a lawyer who finds clients at the scenes of accidents or other tragedies, especially with the intention of earning money through legal action

examples.x: I don't trust those ambulance chasers who keep calling us after the car accident.
He's been labeled as an ambulance chaser because he's always the first one at the scene of an accident trying to sign up clients.
It's unfortunate that some lawyers give the entire profession a bad name by behaving like ambulance chasers
She accused the lawyer of being an ambulance chaser when he showed up at her hospital room immediately after her fall.

senses.definition: to do something unfair in order to make a particular outcome more probable

examples.x: He tried to load the dice by giving the exam questions to his friends in advance, but they got caught cheating.
The company's executives were accused of loading the dice by fixing the bidding process to favor a particular supplier.
It's not fair to load the dice in a competition; everyone should have an equal chance to win.
The coach was criticized for trying to load the dice by selecting the easiest opponents for the team.
In a fair negotiation, neither party should attempt to load the dice by withholding important information.

word_list.word: [set] a thief to catch a thief
word_list.pronunciation: sˈɛt ɐ θˈiːf tə kˈætʃ ɐ θˈiːf

senses.definition: to make use of a thief or criminal just to be able to anticipate, understand, or capture other criminals or thieves

examples.x: To catch the art forger, the museum hired an expert art authenticator, believing that 'setting a thief to catch a thief' would be the best approach.
The detective sets a thief to catch a thief by recruiting an ex-convict to gather information on a notorious gang.
The police department set a thief to catch a thief when they enlisted a reformed burglar to help solve a string of unsolved robberies.
In their efforts to combat art forgery, the museum is considering setting a thief to catch a thief by consulting a skilled art forger for insights into detecting counterfeit artworks.

senses.definition: to arrange situations or conditions to achieve a preferred result by giving oneself an unfair advantage

examples.x: The manager stacked the deck by giving all the best assignments to his favorite employees.
It's clear that they've stacked the cards against the smaller businesses, making it impossible for them to compete.
In the political race, the incumbent candidate seemed to have stacked the deck in his favor with extensive campaign funding.
To ensure their team's victory, the coach was accused of stacking the cards by recruiting the best players from other schools.
The company's management had stacked the deck by making it nearly impossible for employees to unionize.

word_list.pronunciation: lˈiːd ˌɛsbˈiː ɐɹˈaʊnd baɪ ðə nˈoʊz

senses.definition: to manipulate or control someone easily, typically by making them obediently follow one's commands or desires

examples.x: The strict teacher had the entire class under control, leading them by the nose and ensuring their compliance with his rules.
She had a way of leading him around by the nose, making him do whatever she wanted.

senses.definition: to use someone else's idea, plan, words, etc. in order to prevent them from reaching success or getting attention

examples.x: I spent weeks working on that presentation, and my colleague just stole my thunder by presenting my ideas as if they were his own.
He was about to reveal his groundbreaking invention, but his competitor stole his thunder by announcing a similar product a day earlier.
The student worked hard on a project, but his classmate stole his thunder by presenting a more polished version.
The team had a great idea, but the manager stole their thunder by presenting it to the company's executives without giving them credit.

senses.definition: to engage in activities or behaviors that involve deception, manipulation, or deceit in order to achieve a particular outcome, often at the expense of others

examples.x: I can't trust him because he always plays games with people's feelings.
She's not interested in a serious relationship; she just wants to play games.
In negotiations, it's important not to play games and be transparent about your intentions.

senses.definition: to convince someone to do something that seems unnecessary or redundant, especially to buy something they do not need

examples.x: John could sell ice to Eskimos; he's an incredibly persuasive salesperson.
She tried to sell me a new phone even though I already have the latest model—it's like selling ice to Eskimos.
His charm and charisma are so convincing that he could sell ice to Eskimos if he wanted to.
Don't waste your time trying to sell that product here; it's like selling ice to Eskimos.
The marketing team managed to sell the idea to the CEO, but convincing the customers will be like selling ice to Eskimos.
